message.content:
## Web Searcher: time-scale modification algorithms

Research question: "Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible."

Your angle: **time-scale modification algorithms** — Core DSP techniques that compress duration while preserving pitch, the basis of speed-listening playback.
Search query: `WSOLA PSOLA phase vocoder time-scale modification speech speed-up algorithm comparison intelligibility`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
Web search results for query: "WSOLA PSOLA phase vocoder time-scale modification speech speed-up algorithm comparison intelligibility"

Links: [{"title":"[PDF] A SHAPE-INVARIANT PHASE VOCODER FOR SPEECH TRANSFORMATION | Semantic Scholar","url":"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/A-SHAPE-INVARIANT-PHASE-VOCODER-FOR-SPEECH-R%C3%B6bel/c4e7f73985ab19f770d12013882b1f484b1f57e0"},{"title":"An Objective Output Quality Comparison | Download Table","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/figure/An-Objective-Output-Quality-Comparison_tbl2_228766312"},{"title":"(PDF) A Shape-Invariant Phase Vocoder for Speech Transformation","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/280894512_A_Shape-Invariant_Phase_Vocoder_for_Speech_Transformation"},{"title":"Frequency Dependent Time-Scale Modification","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/330875765_Frequency_Dependent_Time-Scale_Modification"},{"title":"Non-uniform time scale modification using instants of significant excitation and vowel onset points - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639313000344"},{"title":"The Overview of Segmental Durations Modification Algorithms on Speech Signal Characteristics","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2507.04264"},{"title":"PVSOLA: A Phase Vocoder with Synchronized OverLap-Add","url":"http://recherche.ircam.fr/pub/dafx11/Papers/57_e.pdf"},{"title":"Enhancing speech intelligibility using variable-rate time-scale modification","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/7065485"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding these time-scale modification algorithms:

## Algorithm Characteristics

WSOLA does not require pitch marking which makes it more suitable for real-time applications compared to PSOLA. WSOLA overcomes distortions of OLA by selecting the segment for overlap-addition, within a given tolerance of the target position, such that the synthesized waveform has maximal similarity to the original signal across segment boundaries.

The phase vocoder is a well established tool for time scaling and pitch shifting speech and audio signals via modification of their short-time Fourier transforms (STFTs). Additionally, the phase vocoder has the advantage to work with both mono and polyphonic signals and theoretically overlaps frames perfectly in phase with each other.

## Performance Comparison

Among WSOLA, SOLAFS, and Phase Vocoder algorithms, WSOLA algorithm has the highest performance while SOLAFS and Phase Vocoder algorithm are more computationally efficient. More specifically, SOLAFS has greater energy loss and difference in power spectrum density than WSOLA, but it was more computationally efficient than WSOLA as the execution time was less than half of the WSOLA's.

## Intelligibility Findings

Regarding intelligibility, ESOLA significantly outperforms the conventional pitch synchronous overlap-add (PSOLA) techniques in terms of perceptual quality and intelligibility of the modified speech.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "time-scale modification speech intelligibility WSOLA phase vocoder review evaluation"

Links: [{"title":"[PDF] A Review of Time-Scale Modification of Music Signals † | Semantic Scholar","url":"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/A-Review-of-Time-Scale-Modification-of-Music-%E2%80%A0-Driedger-M%C3%BCller/0ac0fed7476412c7d85073e1cc2dd0923a1ce90f"},{"title":"Adaptive Time-Scale Modification for Improving Speech Intelligibility Based On Phoneme Clustering For Streaming Services","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/374947761_Adaptive_Time-Scale_Modification_for_Improving_Speech_Intelligibility_Based_On_Phoneme_Clustering_For_Streaming_Services"},{"title":"[PDF] Improved phase vocoder time-scale modification of audio | Semantic Scholar","url":"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Improved-phase-vocoder-time-scale-modification-of-Laroche-Dolson/8312d42cab3f14152d8e6406a9c0463737b6aa45"},{"title":"Frequency Dependent Time-Scale Modification","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/330875765_Frequency_Dependent_Time-Scale_Modification"},{"title":"(PDF) Time-Scale Modification of Speech Signals","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/3320737_Time-Scale_Modification_of_Speech_Signals"},{"title":"(PDF) Time-Scale Modification of Audio Signals Using Enhanced WSOLA With Management of Transients","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/3457965_Time-Scale_Modification_of_Audio_Signals_Using_Enhanced_WSOLA_With_Management_of_Transients"},{"title":"A flexible bit rate switching method for low bit rate vocoders - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S016516840100072X"},{"title":"Enhancing speech intelligibility using variable-rate time-scale modification","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/7065485"},{"title":"SELEBI: Percussion-aware Time Stretching via Selective Magnitude Spectrogram Compression by Nonstationary Gabor Transform","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.16421"}]

I've completed the web search for your query on time-scale modification, speech intelligibility, WSOLA, phase vocoder, and evaluation methods. Here are the key findings:

## Time-Scale Modification Techniques

The WSOLA (waveform-similarity-based synchronized overlap-add) algorithm produces high-quality speech output, is algorithmically and computationally efficient and robust, and allows for online processing with arbitrary time-scaling factors that may be specified in a time-varying fashion.

The phase vocoder is a well established tool for time scaling and pitch shifting speech and audio signals via modification of their short-time Fourier transforms (STFTs), and is generally considered to yield high quality results, especially for large modification factors and/or polyphonic signals.

## Known Artifacts and Improvements

The phase vocoder is known for introducing a characteristic perceptual artifact, often described as "phasiness", "reverberation", or "loss of presence", and two extensions to the standard phase vocoder algorithm have been introduced to significantly improve the resulting sound quality.

Time-scale modifications of speech signals, based on frequency-domain techniques, are hampered by two important artifacts which are "phasiness" and "transient smearing".

## Speech Intelligibility Evaluation

WSOLA algorithm preserves qualities such as naturalness, intelligibility and speaker dependent features such as pitch and formant structure after time-scale modification.

ESOLA significantly outperforms the conventional pitch synchronous overlap-add (PSOLA) techniques in terms of perceptual quality and intelligibility of the modified speech.

The search results indicate active research in this area with various evaluation methods including subjective listening tests and perceptual assessments.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: Let me run two more targeted searches to cover the "speed listening" / extreme speed-up and intelligibility-enhancement angles, plus the canonical Driedger & Müller review which is the best survey of TSM algorithms.

accelerated speech comprehension perceptual limits blind users speed listening training adaptation fast speech

content.content:
Web search results for query: "accelerated speech comprehension perceptual limits blind users speed listening training adaptation fast speech"

Links: [{"title":"Frontiers | Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full"},{"title":"Comprehension of ultra-fast speech-blind vs.\" normally hearing\" persons | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228846770_Comprehension_of_ultra-fast_speech-blind_vs_normally_hearing_persons"},{"title":"(PDF) Maximum listening speeds for the blind","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/242609799_Maximum_listening_speeds_for_the_blind"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3805979/"},{"title":"Network Modeling for Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) Signals during Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension in Late-Blind Listeners","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4492787/"},{"title":"What determines the speed of speech recognition? Evidence from congenitally blind adults - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0028393218300897"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what research has found about accelerated speech comprehension, perceptual limits, and blind users:

## Perceptual Limits and Capabilities

Vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second—exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at approximately 8 syllables per second. In one study, comprehension of ultra-fast speech utterances (16 syl/s) extended in blind listeners from 0 to 93% performance, while in sighted individuals performance consistently fell below 16%.

## Training and Adaptation

Research has examined whether acquisition of ultra-fast speech perception skills induces new activation in the central-visual system in late-blind participants, and to what extent subjects with normal or residual vision can improve understanding of accelerated verbal utterances through specific training measures.

## Practical Applications

This exceptional skill allows for the processing of large amounts of written materials using screen-reading text-to-speech devices and may help, e.g., to better cope with the demands of college or university education.

## Neural Mechanisms

Blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant brain activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex, contralateral fusiform gyrus, and bilateral pulvinar, suggesting that the central-visual system contributes to the enhanced processing of non-visual stimuli in blind subjects.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "Driedger Müller review time-scale modification music signals OLA WSOLA phase vocoder tutorial"

Links: [{"title":"(PDF) A Review of Time-Scale Modification of Music Signals","url":"https://www.academia.edu/83013317/A_Review_of_Time_Scale_Modification_of_Music_Signals?uc-sb-sw=58424559"},{"title":"GitHub - KAIST-MACLab/PyTSMod: An open-source Python library for audio time-scale modification. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/KAIST-MACLab/PyTSMod"},{"title":"[PDF] A Review of Time-Scale Modification of Music Signals † | Semantic Scholar","url":"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/A-Review-of-Time-Scale-Modification-of-Music-%E2%80%A0-Driedger-M%C3%BCller/0ac0fed7476412c7d85073e1cc2dd0923a1ce90f"},{"title":"A Review of Time-Scale Modification of Music Signals","url":"https://dx.doi.org/10.3390/app6020057"},{"title":"pytsmod · PyPI","url":"https://pypi.org/project/pytsmod/"},{"title":"A Review of Time-Scale Modification of Music Signals","url":"https://audiolabs-erlangen.de/content/05_fau/professor/00_mueller/06_projects/90_siamus/2016_DriedgerMueller_TSMOverview_AppliedSciences_ePrint.pdf"},{"title":"AudioLabs - TSM toolbox","url":"https://www.audiolabs-erlangen.de/resources/MIR/TSMtoolbox/"},{"title":"SELEBI: Percussion-aware Time Stretching via Selective Magnitude Spectrogram Compression by Nonstationary Gabor Transform","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.16421"}]

## Search Results

I found relevant results for your query about time-scale modification of music signals. Here's what the search returned:

### Key Publications

Jonathan Driedger and Meinard Müller published "A review of time-scale modification of music signals" in Applied Sciences, 6(2), 57, 2016. This appears to be a comprehensive review covering the main techniques in the field.

### Algorithms Covered

The review covers basic TSM algorithms such as Overlap-Add (OLA), Waveform-Similarity Overlap-Add (WSOLA), Time-Domain Pitch-Synchronous Overlap-Add (TD-PSOLA), and Phase Vocoder (PV-TSM).

### TSM Toolbox

The TSM toolbox was developed by Jonathan Driedger and Meinard Müller and contains MATLAB implementations of various classical time-scale modification (TSM) algorithms like OLA, WSOLA, and the phase vocoder. The toolbox also provides code for a TSM algorithm based on a combination of classical algorithms and harmonic-percussive source separation (HPSS).

### Related Work

Driedger, Müller, and Ewert published research on "Improving time-scale modification of music signals using harmonic-percussive separation" in IEEE Signal Processing Letters, 2014, 21, 105-109.

The search also found open-source implementations like PyTSMod, a Python library that implements these algorithms for easy access and experimentation.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: I have strong coverage across all angles. Let me return the top results, ranked by relevance to the original question (which combines TSM DSP algorithms with the "speed listening" / extreme speed-up perceptual angle).

results.title: A Review of Time-Scale Modification of Music Signals (Driedger & Müller, Applied Sciences 2016)
results.snippet: The canonical, accessible survey of TSM. Explains and compares the three core families that underpin speed-listening playback: Overlap-Add (OLA), Waveform-Similarity Overlap-Add (WSOLA), TD-PSOLA, and the Phase Vocoder (PV-TSM), including how each compresses duration while preserving pitch and the artifacts (transient smearing, phasiness) that limit intelligibility at high factors. Best single starting point; also pairs with the authors' open MATLAB TSM toolbox and the PyTSMod Python library for hands-on implementation.

results.title: Time-Scale Modification of Speech Signals (overview / WSOLA foundations)
results.snippet: Directly addresses speech (not just music). Describes why WSOLA produces high-quality output, is computationally efficient and robust, supports online processing with arbitrary and time-varying scaling factors, and preserves naturalness, intelligibility, pitch and formant structure after time-scaling. Explains how WSOLA improves on plain OLA by waveform-similarity segment selection, and why it avoids the pitch-marking requirement of PSOLA — making it the practical default for real-time speed-up players.

results.title: Training of Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension Induces Functional Reorganization in Late-Blind Humans (Frontiers / PMC)
results.snippet: Directly answers the 'speed listening above 16x' perceptual-limits angle. Reports that trained blind listeners comprehend accelerated speech up to ~22 syllables/s versus ~8 syl/s for sighted listeners, and that at 16 syl/s blind comprehension reached 93% while sighted stayed below 16%. Documents the training/adaptation effect and the neural reorganization (recruitment of visual cortex) that makes extreme speed-ups intelligible — the human-side counterpart to the DSP methods.

results.title: PVSOLA: A Phase Vocoder with Synchronized OverLap-Add (IRCAM, DAFx-11)
results.snippet: Shows the state of the art that hybridizes the two main TSM families: it injects WSOLA-style synchronized overlap-add into the phase vocoder to reset accumulated phase error, directly attacking the 'phasiness'/loss-of-presence artifact that degrades intelligibility of phase-vocoder time-stretching at large factors. Relevant for understanding how modern players push speed-up quality higher.

results.title: Improved Phase-Vocoder Time-Scale Modification of Audio (Laroche & Dolson)
results.snippet: Foundational phase-vocoder paper. Identifies the characteristic phase-vocoder artifact ('phasiness'/reverberation/loss of presence) that limits comprehensibility at high stretch factors, and introduces phase-locking extensions that significantly improve output quality. Essential background for the spectral-domain branch of speed-listening DSP and why naive STFT time-stretching sounds smeared.

results.title: The Overview of Segmental Durations Modification Algorithms on Speech Signal Characteristics (arXiv 2025)
results.snippet: Recent comparative evaluation benchmarking WSOLA, SOLAFS, and the phase vocoder on speech. Finds WSOLA gives the highest quality (least energy loss / power-spectral-density distortion) while SOLAFS and the phase vocoder are more computationally efficient (SOLAFS runs in under half WSOLA's time). Useful, up-to-date quantitative comparison for choosing an algorithm for fast playback, including notes on ESOLA outperforming PSOLA on perceptual quality and intelligibility.

content.text:
I've completed the research on time-scale modification algorithms and returned the top 6 results via StructuredOutput, ranked by relevance to the original question (which spans both the DSP/TSM algorithm angle and the "speed listening" perceptual-limits angle).

Key coverage:
- The Driedger & Müller 2016 review is the authoritative survey of the core TSM families (OLA, WSOLA, TD-PSOLA, phase vocoder) and is freely available as a PDF.
- A speech-specific TSM overview establishes why WSOLA is the practical default for real-time speed-up while preserving pitch/formants/intelligibility.
- The Frontiers/PMC ultra-fast speech study directly answers the >16x perceptual-limits and training/adaptation angle (blind listeners reaching ~22 syl/s vs ~8 for sighted).
- PVSOLA and the Laroche & Dolson phase-vocoder paper cover the spectral-domain branch and the phasiness artifact that limits comprehensibility at high stretch factors.
- A 2025 arXiv comparative evaluation provides recent quantitative WSOLA vs SOLAFS vs phase-vocoder benchmarks.
